title: In-Context Learning under Distribution Shift: Optimal Attention Temperature for Transformers

reviews.summary: This paper studies how the attention temperature in Transformers affects in-context learning (ICL) robustness under distribution shift. The authors introduce a “linearized softmax” attention that preserves normalization and explicit temperature dependence while remaining tractable, derive a closed-form generalization error (Theorem 4.6) and an explicit expression for the optimal test-time temperature that minimizes this error (Theorem 4.7), and validate the theory with simulations on linear regression prompts and with LLaMA2-7B on question answering under label-noise shifts. The central finding is that tuning attention temperature is a principled and effective lever to mitigate distribution-shift degradation in ICL.
strengths: Introduces a linearized softmax attention model that retains temperature’s role and normalization, enabling closed-form analysis while remaining closer to softmax than standard linear attention.
Derives an explicit analytical expression for the ICL generalization error and a closed-form optimal temperature that minimizes it under broad Gaussian-task assumptions.
Provides a clear link between types of shift (covariance, task prior, noise) and the optimal temperature, including insightful special cases (e.g., isotropic covariance scaling).
Synthetic linear-regression experiments align closely with the theory across multiple shift types, demonstrating the predictive value of the analysis.

LLM experiments (LLaMA2-7B on SCIQ with noisy in-context examples) corroborate the predicted trend that higher test-time noise favors larger attention temperature, and illustrate a practical performance benefit.
Problem setup and assumptions are clearly stated; the role of reparameterized attention blocks in the prediction is well explained.
The decomposition of distribution-shift cases and their qualitative impact on ICL performance is easy to follow.
Addresses an important and practical question—robust ICL under shift—with a simple intervention (tuning τ) that practitioners can use immediately.
Complements and extends prior analyses of linear attention by bringing temperature (and softmax-like normalization) explicitly into the ICL robustness story.
weaknesses: The validity regime of the “linearized softmax” approximation is not fully characterized in the main text. Conditions under which the first-order expansion and normalization correction remain accurate (e.g., bounds on logit variance/scale) are not clearly stated.

The optimal-τ formula requires positivity conditions on trace terms; sufficient conditions ensuring these hold under realistic pretraining/test distributions are not provided, leaving ambiguity about failure modes (e.g., denominator near zero).

Pretraining lemma assumes access to or accurate estimation of σ2, μw, Σw and uses a closed-form parameter choice; applicability to practical pretraining (with stochastic optimizers, deep stacks, and non-Gaussian data) is discussed only qualitatively.

LLM validation is limited in scope: one model family (LLaMA2-7B), one dataset (SCIQ), and a single type of shift (noisy labels). No comparison to alternative, simple robustness interventions (e.g., layer-wise scaling, attention mask reweighting, prompt selection or order strategies) is provided.
GPT-2 results are deferred to the appendix; the main text would benefit from at least one non-toy deep-model result beyond a single LLM/dataset to strengthen generality claims.
Minor typesetting artifacts (e.g., stray prime in Eq. (15), formatting glitches) and placeholder characters in Related Work may distract.

The practical recipe to estimate or approximate τoptimal at inference in large models is not prominently documented in the main text (e.g., from observable statistics), though this is central for deployment.

Limited discussion of distributionally robust ICL and robustness scaling (e.g., Wasserstein DRO perspectives on ICL robustness) and of recent analyses comparing softmax vs. linearized attention in retrieval/regression settings.
Connections to recent works that interpret softmax temperature as a bandwidth or preconditioning control in ICL could be discussed in more depth.
reviews.questions:
1. Linearized softmax approximation: Around which operating point is the softmax linearized, and under what quantitative conditions (e.g., bounds on the variance of QK⊤/τ) is the approximation accurate? Can you provide error bounds or empirical diagnostics indicating when Eq. (5) remains predictive?
2. Practical estimation of τoptimal: In large pretrained models, how do you estimate or approximate the traces in Theorem 4.7 without access to ground-truth A, B, and the trained M, V blocks? Do you recommend a concrete, model-internal statistic-based heuristic (beyond grid search), and how sensitive are results to that choice?
3. Positivity conditions: Can you provide sufficient conditions (on Σx, Σw, v21, v22, M11) that guarantee the numerator and denominator in Eq. (15) are positive, or propose a robust fallback when the denominator is small/negative (e.g., clipping, constrained search)?
4. Multi-head and deep stacks: Does your analysis extend in a straightforward way to multi-head attention (e.g., block-diagonal M11 per head) and multiple layers (compounding temperatures)? Is a single global τ per layer near-optimal, or do you observe benefits from per-head τ in practice?
5. Beyond Gaussian shifts: Have you tested the τ-tuning heuristic under non-Gaussian or heavy-tailed shifts (e.g., outliers, adversarial perturbations)? How robust are the qualitative predictions (e.g., τ increasing with noise) when the assumptions are violated?
6. LLM experiments: How was τ selected across layers and heads in LLaMA2-7B—uniformly or proportionally to layer statistics? Did you observe any optimization or stability issues (e.g., attention saturation, gradient anomalies in residual streams) when increasing τ at inference?
7. Baselines: Could you compare against other low-cost robustness adjustments (e.g., per-layer rescaling, calibrated attention masks, prompt-order optimization) to contextualize the magnitude of improvements from τ tuning?
reviews.assessment: This paper addresses an important and timely question—how to improve ICL robustness under distribution shift—through a theoretically grounded analysis of attention temperature. The linearized softmax construction is a thoughtful compromise that preserves temperature’s operational role and yields closed-form insights, including an explicit τoptimal. The synthetic experiments convincingly validate the theory, and the LLaMA2-7B results demonstrate practical relevance. The main limitations are the scope of the linearization (with limited discussion of validity regimes), relatively narrow LLM evaluation, and the absence of comparisons to alternative simple robustness interventions. Despite these, the contribution is sufficiently novel and useful: a principled treatment of attention temperature for ICL under shift, with actionable guidance and initial evidence of transfer to real models. I lean toward acceptance, recommending that the authors (i) clarify the approximation regime and positivity conditions, (ii) document a practical τ-selection heuristic in the main text, and (iii) broaden empirical validation or baselines if space permits.
